Let $G$ denote a set of invertible $2 \times 2$ matrices (matrices with complex
numbers as entries and determinant nonzero) with the property that if $a, b$
are in $G$, then so are $ab$ and $a^{-1}$. Suppose there exists a function $f :
G \to \mathbb{R}$ with the property that either $f(ga) > f(a)$ or
$f(g^{-1} a) > f(a)$ for all $a, g$ in $G$ with $g \ne I$ (here $I$ denotes the
identity matrix, $\mathbb{R}$ denotes the real numbers, and the inequality
signs are strict inequality). Prove that given finite nonempty subsets $A, B$
of $G$, there is a matrix in $G$ which can be written in exactly one way in the
form $xy$ with $x$ in $A$ and $y$ in $B$.
